content:
# Exodus Chapter 11 — The EMET Bible (Literal)

**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios ~38r–38v (PDF pp. 72–73); Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 51–52 (LXX) | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_011_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_011_page_002.jpg`; `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/EXO_011_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_011_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

## Announcement of the Final Plague — Nega Echad (vv. 1–10)

**1** And YHWH said to Mosheh: "One more nega [plague/blow] I will bring upon Par'oh and upon Mitsrayim; afterward he will send you out from here. When he sends [you] out completely, he will surely drive you out from here.

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוָה אֶל־מֹשֶׁה עוֹד נֶגַע אֶחָד אָבִיא עַל־פַּרְעֹה וְעַל־מִצְרַיִם אַחֲרֵי־כֵן יְשַׁלַּח אֶתְכֶם מִזֶּה כְּשַׁלְּחוֹ כָּלָה גָּרֵשׁ יְגָרֵשׁ אֶתְכֶם מִזֶּה (od nega ekhad avi al-Par'oh ve-al-Mitsrayim, "one more plague/blow I will bring upon Par'oh and upon Mitsrayim"; akharei-khen yeshalakh etkhem mi-zeh, "afterward he will send you out from here"; ke-shalkho kalah garesh yegaresh etkhem mi-zeh, "when he sends [you] out completely, he will surely drive you out from here"). **Key term:** נֶגַע (nega) = "plague, blow, stroke" — from נגע (naga, "to touch, strike"). This is not the more common word for plague (מַגֵּפָה, magefah) but a word emphasizing a striking blow. The phrase נֶגַע אֶחָד (nega ekhad, "one plague/blow") signals finality — one single, decisive blow remains. The infinitive absolute construction גָּרֵשׁ יְגָרֵשׁ (garesh yegaresh, "he will surely drive out") intensifies: Par'oh will not merely release but forcibly expel them. The verb גרשׁ (garash, "to drive out") echoes 10:11 where Par'oh drove Mosheh from his presence — now Par'oh will drive all Yisra'el from his land. LXX (Vaticanus): εἶπεν δὲ κύριος πρὸς Μωυσῆν· ἔτι μίαν πληγὴν ἐπάξω ἐπὶ Φαραω καὶ ἐπ᾽ Αἴγυπτον, καὶ μετὰ ταῦτα ἐξαποστελεῖ ὑμᾶς ἐντεῦθεν ("and the Lord said to Moses: yet one plague I will bring upon Pharao and upon Egypt, and after this he will send you out from here") — πληγή ("blow, plague") for nega.

**2** Speak, please, in the ears of the people, and let them ask [sha'al], each man from his neighbor and each woman from her neighbor, articles of silver and articles of gold."

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads דַּבֶּר־נָא בְּאָזְנֵי הָעָם וְיִשְׁאֲלוּ אִישׁ מֵאֵת רֵעֵהוּ וְאִשָּׁה מֵאֵת רְעוּתָהּ כְּלֵי־כֶסֶף וּכְלֵי זָהָב (dabber-na be-oznei ha-am, "speak, please, in the ears of the people"; ve-yish'alu ish me-et re'ehu ve-ishah me-et re'utah, "and let them ask, each man from his neighbor and each woman from her neighbor"; kelei-khesef u-khlei zahav, "articles of silver and articles of gold"). **Key verb — the "asking/borrowing" controversy:** וְיִשְׁאֲלוּ (ve-yish'alu) from שָׁאַל (sha'al, "to ask, request, inquire"). The verb sha'al does NOT mean "borrow" — it means "ask" or "request." There is no implication of return. The traditional rendering "borrow" (from KJV) is misleading. The Israelites are to ask for — and receive — the wealth of Mitsrayim. This fulfills the promise to Avraham in Bereshit 15:14: "afterward they shall come out with great substance" (bi-rekhush gadol). The word כְּלֵי (kelei) = "articles, vessels, implements" — from כְּלִי (keli). The phrase בְּאָזְנֵי הָעָם (be-oznei ha-am, "in the ears of the people") echoes 10:2 — again, communication is oral, "in the ears." LXX: λάλησον οὖν κρυφῇ εἰς τὰ ὦτα τοῦ λαοῦ, καὶ αἰτησάτω ἕκαστος παρὰ τοῦ πλησίον καὶ γυνὴ παρὰ τῆς πλησίον σκεύη ἀργυρᾶ καὶ χρυσᾶ ("speak therefore secretly in the ears of the people, and let each one ask from his neighbor, and each woman from her neighbor, articles of silver and gold") — αἰτέω ("ask, request") for sha'al; note LXX adds κρυφῇ ("secretly"), not present in MT.

**3** And YHWH gave the chen [favor/grace] of the people in the eyes of Mitsrayim. Also, the man Mosheh [was] very great in the land of Mitsrayim, in the eyes of the servants of Par'oh and in the eyes of the people.

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיִּתֵּן יְהוָה אֶת־חֵן הָעָם בְּעֵינֵי מִצְרָיִם גַּם הָאִישׁ מֹשֶׁה גָּדוֹל מְאֹד בְּאֶרֶץ מִצְרַיִם בְּעֵינֵי עַבְדֵי־פַרְעֹה וּבְעֵינֵי הָעָם (vayitten YHWH et-khen ha-am be-einei Mitsrayim, "and YHWH gave the favor of the people in the eyes of Mitsrayim"; gam ha-ish Mosheh gadol me'od be-erets Mitsrayim, "also the man Mosheh [was] very great in the land of Mitsrayim"; be-einei avdei-Par'oh u-ve-einei ha-am, "in the eyes of the servants of Par'oh and in the eyes of the people"). **Key phrase — "the man Mosheh was very great":** This is an extraordinary parenthetical — a rare editorial aside within a divine speech. The word חֵן (khen) = "grace, favor, charm" — YHWH himself engineers Egyptian goodwill toward the Israelites. The phrase הָאִישׁ מֹשֶׁה (ha-ish Mosheh, "the man Mosheh") is a notable construction — Mosheh is described with the simple word אִישׁ (ish, "man"), yet his stature is גָּדוֹל מְאֹד (gadol me'od, "very great"). This contrasts with Mosheh's self-description as "not a man of words" (4:10) and "heavy of mouth" (4:10, kaved — the same root as the hardening!). The word בְּעֵינֵי (be-einei, "in the eyes of") appears three times in this verse — the Egyptian gaze upon Yisra'el has shifted from contempt to awe. LXX: καὶ ἔδωκεν κύριος τὴν χάριν τῷ λαῷ αὐτοῦ ἐναντίον τῶν Αἰγυπτίων... καὶ ὁ ἄνθρωπος Μωυσῆς μέγας ἐγενήθη σφόδρα ("and the Lord gave favor to his people before the Egyptians... and the man Moses became exceedingly great") — χάρις ("grace, favor") for khen; μέγας σφόδρα ("exceedingly great") for gadol me'od.

**4** And Mosheh said: "Thus says YHWH: 'About the middle of the night I [am] going out in the midst of Mitsrayim.

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיֹּאמֶר מֹשֶׁה כֹּה אָמַר יְהוָה כַּחֲצֹת הַלַּיְלָה אֲנִי יוֹצֵא בְּתוֹךְ מִצְרָיִם (koh amar YHWH, "thus says YHWH"; ka-khatsot ha-lailah, "about the middle/dividing of the night"; ani yotse be-tokh Mitsrayim, "I [am] going out in the midst of Mitsrayim"). **Key phrase — YHWH goes out:** The participle יוֹצֵא (yotse, "going out") describes YHWH's own movement through the land — not sending an angel or an agent but personally traversing Mitsrayim. The word חֲצֹת (khatsot) from חצה (khatsah, "to divide, halve") — "the dividing of the night," i.e., midnight. The preposition כַּ (ka-, "about, approximately") introduces a slight imprecision that heightens dread — the exact moment is known only to YHWH. **Narrative placement:** This speech (vv. 4–8) is likely delivered in Par'oh's presence before Mosheh's departure in 10:29 — it is the final word Mosheh speaks before Par'oh. LXX: εἶπεν δὲ Μωυσῆς· τάδε λέγει κύριος· περὶ μέσας νύκτας ἐγὼ εἰσπορεύομαι εἰς μέσον Αἰγύπτου ("and Moses said: thus says the Lord: about the middle of the night I go into the midst of Egypt") — εἰσπορεύομαι ("go into") for yotse ("go out"); note the Greek reverses the direction — "going into" rather than "going out."

**5** And every bekhor [firstborn] in the land of Mitsrayim shall die, from the bekhor of Par'oh who sits upon his throne unto the bekhor of the slave-woman who [is] behind the mill, and every bekhor of cattle.

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וּמֵת כָּל־בְּכוֹר בְּאֶרֶץ מִצְרַיִם מִבְּכוֹר פַּרְעֹה הַיֹּשֵׁב עַל־כִּסְאוֹ עַד בְּכוֹר הַשִּׁפְחָה אֲשֶׁר אַחַר הָרֵחָיִם וְכֹל בְּכוֹר בְּהֵמָה (u-met kol-bekhor be-erets Mitsrayim, "and every firstborn in the land of Mitsrayim shall die"; mi-bekhor Par'oh ha-yoshev al-kis'o, "from the firstborn of Par'oh who sits upon his throne"; ad bekhor ha-shifkhah asher akhar ha-rekhayim, "unto the firstborn of the slave-woman who [is] behind the mill"; ve-khol bekhor behemah, "and every firstborn of cattle"). **Key term:** בְּכוֹר (bekhor) = "firstborn" — from בכר (bakhar, "to be firstborn, bear early fruit"). The firstborn holds unique status in ANE culture: heir, family representative, bearer of blessing. The range "from Par'oh's firstborn... to the slave-woman's firstborn" is a merism — from the highest to the lowest, no household is exempt. The word רֵחָיִם (rekhayim) = "mill, millstones" — a dual form, referring to the two stones of a hand-mill. The slave-woman grinding at the mill is the lowest social position in Egypt. The word שִׁפְחָה (shifkhah) = "slave-woman, maidservant." The word בְּהֵמָה (behemah) = "cattle, beast" — even animals are included in the judgment. LXX: καὶ τελευτήσει πᾶν πρωτότοκον ἐν γῇ Αἰγύπτῳ, ἀπὸ πρωτοτόκου Φαραω ὃς κάθηται ἐπὶ τοῦ θρόνου, ἕως πρωτοτόκου τῆς θεραπαίνης τῆς παρὰ τὸν μύλον, καὶ ἕως πρωτοτόκου παντὸς κτήνους ("and every firstborn in the land of Egypt shall die, from the firstborn of Pharao who sits upon the throne, to the firstborn of the maidservant who is by the mill, and to the firstborn of every beast") — πρωτότοκος ("firstborn") for bekhor; μύλος ("millstone") for rekhayim.

**6** And there shall be a tse'aqah gedolah [great cry] in all the land of Mitsrayim, which like it has not been, and like it shall not again be.

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וְהָיְתָה צְעָקָה גְדֹלָה בְּכָל־אֶרֶץ מִצְרָיִם אֲשֶׁר כָּמֹהוּ לֹא נִהְיָתָה וְכָמֹהוּ לֹא תֹסִף (ve-hayetah tse'aqah gedolah be-khol-erets Mitsrayim, "and there shall be a great cry in all the land of Mitsrayim"; asher kamohu lo nihyatah, "which like it has not been"; ve-khamohu lo tosif, "and like it shall not again [be]"). **Key phrase — tse'aqah gedolah:** צְעָקָה (tse'aqah) = "cry, outcry, shriek" — from צעק (tsa'aq, "to cry out"). This is the same word used for Yisra'el's cry under slavery (3:7, 9) — now it is Mitsrayim who cries out. The word גְּדֹלָה (gedolah) = "great." The reversal is devastating: the oppressor's cry now matches the cry of the oppressed. The uniqueness formula (like it has not been / shall not again be) echoes the formulae for the hail (9:18, 24) and locust (10:14) — but here it is not a natural phenomenon but human grief that is unprecedented. LXX: καὶ ἔσται κραυγὴ μεγάλη κατὰ πᾶσαν γῆν Αἰγύπτου, ἥτις τοιαύτη οὐ γέγονεν καὶ τοιαύτη οὐκέτι προστεθήσεται ("and there shall be a great cry throughout all the land of Egypt, such as has not been and such shall not again be added") — κραυγή ("cry, scream") for tse'aqah.

**7** But against all the sons of Yisra'el, lo yecheratz kelev leshono [not a dog shall sharpen/move its tongue], from man unto beast — so that you may know that YHWH makes a distinction [yafleh] between Mitsrayim and between Yisra'el.'

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וּלְכֹל בְּנֵי יִשְׂרָאֵל לֹא יֶחֱרַץ־כֶּלֶב לְשֹׁנוֹ לְמֵאִישׁ וְעַד־בְּהֵמָה לְמַעַן תֵּדְעוּן כִּי יַפְלֶה יְהוָה בֵּין מִצְרַיִם וּבֵין יִשְׂרָאֵל (u-le-khol benei Yisra'el lo yecheratz-kelev leshono, "but to/against all the sons of Yisra'el not a dog shall sharpen its tongue"; le-me-ish ve-ad-behemah, "from man unto beast"; le-ma'an ted'un ki yafleh YHWH bein Mitsrayim u-vein Yisra'el, "so that you may know that YHWH makes a distinction between Mitsrayim and between Yisra'el"). **Key phrase — the dog proverb:** לֹא יֶחֱרַץ־כֶּלֶב לְשֹׁנוֹ (lo yecheratz-kelev leshono) — the verb יֶחֱרַץ (yecheratz) from חרץ (kharats, "to sharpen, cut, decide"). Literally: "not a dog shall sharpen its tongue." The meaning is that not even a dog will growl, bark, or make a threatening sound against any Israelite — total, supernatural peace. The image is proverbial: dogs are the most common nocturnal disturbers, barking and snarling at movement. In the night of Mitsrayim's greatest catastrophe, Yisra'el's neighborhood will be so tranquil that not even a dog stirs. **Key verb — yafleh:** The verb יַפְלֶה (yafleh) from פלה (palah, "to separate, distinguish, make distinct") — YHWH actively distinguishes between the two peoples. This continues the pattern of divine separation seen in 8:22 (Goshen exemption), 9:4 (livestock), 9:26 (hail), 10:23 (darkness/light). The distinction is not natural but theological — YHWH acts differently toward Yisra'el and Mitsrayim. LXX: καὶ πρὸς πάντας τοὺς υἱοὺς Ισραηλ οὐ γρύξει κύων τῇ γλώσσῃ αὐτοῦ ἀπὸ ἀνθρώπου ἕως κτήνους ("but against all the sons of Israel not a dog shall growl with its tongue, from man to beast") — γρύζω ("growl, grumble") for yecheratz; ὅπως εἰδῇς ὅσα παραδοξάσει κύριος ἀνὰ μέσον τῶν Αἰγυπτίων καὶ τοῦ Ισραηλ ("that you may know how greatly the Lord shall make a distinction between the Egyptians and Israel") — παραδοξάζω ("do a marvelous/strange thing") for yafleh.

**8** And all these your servants shall come down to me and shall bow down to me, saying: "Go out — you and all the people who [are] at your feet!" And afterward I will go out.'" And he went out from Par'oh in the heat of anger [khari-af].

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וְיָרְדוּ כָל־עֲבָדֶיךָ אֵלֶּה אֵלַי וְהִשְׁתַּחֲווּ־לִי לֵאמֹר צֵא אַתָּה וְכָל־הָעָם אֲשֶׁר־בְּרַגְלֶיךָ וְאַחֲרֵי־כֵן אֵצֵא וַיֵּצֵא מֵעִם־פַּרְעֹה בָּחֳרִי־אָף (ve-yardu khol-avadeicha elleh elai, "and all these your servants shall come down to me"; ve-hishtakhavu-li le-mor, "and they shall bow down to me, saying"; tse attah ve-khol-ha-am asher-be-ragleicha, "go out — you and all the people who [are] at your feet"; ve-akharei-khen etse, "and afterward I will go out"; vayetse me-im-Par'oh ba-khari-af, "and he went out from Par'oh in the heat of anger"). **Mosheh's boldness before Par'oh:** Mosheh predicts that Par'oh's own courtiers (עֲבָדֶיךָ, avadeicha, "your servants") will come to him and prostrate themselves (הִשְׁתַּחֲווּ, hishtakhavu — hitpael of חוה, "to bow down, prostrate") — a stunning reversal of court protocol. Mosheh pointedly says "to me" (אֵלַי, elai) — to the man Par'oh just banished. The phrase אֲשֶׁר־בְּרַגְלֶיךָ (asher-be-ragleicha, "who [are] at your feet") = those who follow you, your retinue. Note that Mosheh avoids saying Par'oh himself will come — diplomatic restraint even in rage. **Key phrase — khari-af:** בָּחֳרִי־אָף (ba-khari-af) = "in the heat/burning of the nose [anger]." The word חֳרִי (khari) from חרה (kharah, "to burn, be kindled"); אָף (af) = "nose, anger." This is the only time Mosheh is described as angry in the exodus narrative. It is a righteous fury at Par'oh's intransigence in the face of impending catastrophe. LXX: καὶ καταβήσονται πάντες οἱ παῖδές σου οὗτοι πρός με καὶ προσκυνήσουσίν με λέγοντες· ἔξελθε σὺ καὶ πᾶς ὁ λαός σου ("and all these your servants shall come down to me and worship me, saying: go out, you and all your people"); ἐξῆλθεν δὲ Μωυσῆς ἀπὸ Φαραω μετὰ θυμοῦ ("and Moses went out from Pharao with anger") — θυμός ("rage, wrath") for khari-af.

**9** And YHWH said to Mosheh: "Par'oh will not listen to you, so that my moftim [wonders] may be multiplied in the land of Mitsrayim."

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וַיֹּאמֶר יְהוָה אֶל־מֹשֶׁה לֹא־יִשְׁמַע אֲלֵיכֶם פַּרְעֹה לְמַעַן רְבוֹת מוֹפְתַי בְּאֶרֶץ מִצְרָיִם (lo-yishma aleikhem Par'oh, "Par'oh will not listen to you"; le-ma'an revot moftai be-erets Mitsrayim, "so that my wonders may be multiplied in the land of Mitsrayim"). **Key term:** מוֹפְתַי (moftai) = "my wonders/portents" — from מוֹפֵת (mofet, "wonder, sign, portent"). This is distinct from אוֹת (ot, "sign") used in 10:1–2; mofet emphasizes the extraordinary, awe-inspiring nature of the acts. The verb רְבוֹת (revot) = qal infinitive of רבה (ravah, "to be many, multiply") — YHWH's purpose in Par'oh's refusal is the multiplication of wonders. The phrase לְמַעַן (le-ma'an, "so that, in order that") states divine teleology directly: Par'oh's refusal serves YHWH's purpose. This verse frames the entire plague narrative theologically — every refusal has been purposeful. LXX: οὐκ εἰσακούσεται ὑμῶν Φαραω, ἵνα πληθύνω μου τὰ σημεῖα καὶ τὰ τέρατα ἐν γῇ Αἰγύπτῳ ("Pharao will not listen to you, that I may multiply my signs and wonders in the land of Egypt") — note LXX adds σημεῖα καὶ τέρατα ("signs and wonders"), expanding moftai into a hendiadys.

**10** And Mosheh and Aharon did all these moftim [wonders] before Par'oh; and YHWH chizzeq [strengthened] the heart of Par'oh, and he did not send out the sons of Yisra'el from his land.

> **Manuscript note:** LC reads וּמֹשֶׁה וְאַהֲרֹן עָשׂוּ אֶת־כָּל־הַמֹּפְתִים הָאֵלֶּה לִפְנֵי פַרְעֹה וַיְחַזֵּק יְהוָה אֶת־לֵב פַּרְעֹה וְלֹא־שִׁלַּח אֶת־בְּנֵי־יִשְׂרָאֵל מֵאַרְצוֹ (u-Mosheh ve-Aharon asu et-kol-ha-moftim ha-elleh lifnei Par'oh, "and Mosheh and Aharon did all these wonders before Par'oh"; vayekhazzeq YHWH et-lev Par'oh, "and YHWH strengthened the heart of Par'oh"; ve-lo-shilakh et-benei-Yisra'el me-artso, "and he did not send out the sons of Yisra'el from his land"). **CRITICAL — HARDENING VERB: chazaq (חזק) = "strengthen." Piel וַיְחַזֵּק — YHWH is the AGENT.** This is the summary statement for the entire plague cycle (chapters 7–11). The piel form וַיְחַזֵּק (vayekhazzeq) indicates intensive, causative action — YHWH actively and forcefully strengthened Par'oh's heart. The verse functions as a closing frame for the plague narrative: despite all the moftim (wonders), YHWH hardened Par'oh and he did not release Yisra'el. The addition מֵאַרְצוֹ (me-artso, "from his land") is emphatic — Par'oh's refusal extends to expulsion from his territorial domain. LXX: Μωυσῆς δὲ καὶ Ααρων ἐποίησαν πάντα τὰ σημεῖα καὶ τὰ τέρατα ταῦτα ἐν γῇ Αἰγύπτῳ ἐναντίον Φαραω· ἐσκλήρυνεν δὲ κύριος τὴν καρδίαν Φαραω, καὶ οὐκ ἐξαπέστειλεν τοὺς υἱοὺς Ισραηλ ἐκ γῆς Αἰγύπτου ("and Moses and Aaron did all these signs and wonders in the land of Egypt before Pharao; and the Lord hardened the heart of Pharao, and he did not send out the sons of Israel from the land of Egypt") — σκληρύνω ("harden") with κύριος as subject; again σημεῖα καὶ τέρατα for moftim.

---

## Hardening-Verb Tracking — Exodus 11

| Verse | Hebrew Root | Verb Form | Translation | Agent |
|-------|-----------|-----------|-------------|-------|
| 11:10 | **חזק (chazaq)** | **Piel וַיְחַזֵּק** | **"strengthened"** | **YHWH** |

### Cumulative Hardening-Verb Summary (Exodus 7–11)

| Verse | Hebrew Root | Verb Form | Translation | Agent |
|-------|-----------|-----------|-------------|-------|
| 7:3 | קשה (qashah) | Hiphil impf. אַקְשֶׁה | "I will make hard" | YHWH (predictive) |
| 7:13 | חזק (chazaq) | Qal וַיֶּחֱזַק | "was strengthened" | Par'oh's heart (self) |
| 7:14 | כבד (kaved) | Adjective כָּבֵד | "[is] heavy" | Par'oh's heart (state) |
| 7:22 | חזק (chazaq) | Qal וַיֶּחֱזַק | "was strengthened" | Par'oh's heart (self) |
| 8:15 | כבד (kaved) | Hiphil inf. abs. הַכְבֵּד | "he made heavy" | Par'oh (self) |
| 8:19 | חזק (chazaq) | Qal וַיֶּחֱזַק | "was strengthened" | Par'oh's heart (self) |
| 8:32 | כבד (kaved) | Hiphil וַיַּכְבֵּד | "he made heavy" | Par'oh (self) |
| 9:7 | כבד (kaved) | Qal וַיִּכְבַּד | "was heavy" | Par'oh's heart (stative) |
| 9:12 | **חזק (chazaq)** | **Piel וַיְחַזֵּק** | **"strengthened"** | **YHWH (first divine chazaq)** |
| 9:34 | כבד (kaved) | Hiphil וַיַּכְבֵּד | "he made heavy" | Par'oh (self) |
| 9:35 | חזק (chazaq) | Qal וַיֶּחֱזַק | "was strengthened" | Par'oh's heart (self) |
| 10:1 | **כבד (kaved)** | **Hiphil הִכְבַּדְתִּי** | **"I have made heavy"** | **YHWH** |
| 10:20 | **חזק (chazaq)** | **Piel וַיְחַזֵּק** | **"strengthened"** | **YHWH** |
| 10:27 | **חזק (chazaq)** | **Piel וַיְחַזֵּק** | **"strengthened"** | **YHWH** |
| **11:10** | **חזק (chazaq)** | **Piel וַיְחַזֵּק** | **"strengthened"** | **YHWH** |

**Pattern note — SUMMARY HARDENING:** Exodus 11:10 is the capstone of the hardening sequence. The verse serves as a retrospective summary: "Mosheh and Aharon did all these wonders... and YHWH strengthened the heart of Par'oh." From 9:12 onward, every hardening verb has YHWH as agent (9:12, 10:1, 10:20, 10:27, 11:10). The total count: Par'oh hardened himself 6 times (7:13, 7:22, 8:15, 8:19, 8:32, 9:34–35); YHWH hardened Par'oh 5 times (9:12, 10:1, 10:20, 10:27, 11:10); YHWH predicted it once (7:3). The piel form of chazaq (intensive) is used exclusively when YHWH is the agent — never the qal. When Par'oh's heart strengthens itself, the qal (simple) form appears. This grammatical distinction reinforces the theological point: YHWH's hardening is forceful and deliberate; Par'oh's self-hardening was a natural disposition that YHWH then seized upon and intensified.

## Key Lexical Notes — Exodus 11

- **nega echad** (נֶגַע אֶחָד, v. 1): "one plague/blow" — from נגע (naga, "to touch, strike"). The word nega emphasizes physical striking, a blow from YHWH. The numeral אֶחָד (ekhad, "one") underscores finality — one decisive blow remains. This word appears in Leviticus for skin-disease afflictions (nega tsara'at), always denoting a divine touch or stroke.
- **sha'al** (שָׁאַל, v. 2): "ask, request" — NOT "borrow." The Israelites are instructed to ask for silver and gold from their Egyptian neighbors. This is not theft or deception; YHWH engineers Egyptian willingness (v. 3). The fulfillment of Bereshit 15:14, Avraham's covenant promise: "afterward they shall come out with great substance."
- **bekhor** (בְּכוֹר, v. 5): "firstborn" — from בכר ("to be firstborn"). The death of the firstborn strikes at the heart of Egyptian dynastic succession and divine claim: Par'oh himself was considered a god, and his firstborn was the divine heir. The merism "from Par'oh's firstborn to the slave-woman's firstborn" encompasses every social stratum.
- **tse'aqah gedolah** (צְעָקָה גְדֹלָה, v. 6): "great cry/outcry" — the same vocabulary as Yisra'el's cry of suffering (2:23, 3:7, 3:9). The reversal is theologically deliberate: the oppressor now shrieks as the oppressed once did.
- **lo yecheratz kelev leshono** (לֹא יֶחֱרַץ־כֶּלֶב לְשֹׁנוֹ, v. 7): "not a dog shall sharpen its tongue" — a proverb for absolute peace and safety. While Mitsrayim wails, not even a dog disturbs Yisra'el's tranquility. The verb חרץ (kharats) means "to sharpen, cut, be decisive" — the dog's tongue, the instrument of barking and growling, remains still.
- **yafleh** (יַפְלֶה, v. 7): "make a distinction" — from פלה (palah, "to be separate, distinct"). YHWH's active separation between the two peoples is the theological heart of the plague narrative. What appears as natural catastrophe is in fact targeted divine action.
- **khari-af** (חֳרִי־אָף, v. 8): "heat/burning of the nose [anger]" — Mosheh's only display of anger in the plague narrative. The combination of חרה (kharah, "to burn") and אָף (af, "nose/anger") creates a vivid physiological image: the face flushing, nostrils flaring.
- **moftim** (מוֹפְתִים, vv. 9–10): "wonders, portents" — distinct from אוֹתוֹת (otot, "signs"). A mofet is an extraordinary deed that inspires awe and dread; an ot is a sign that communicates meaning. The plagues are both: signs (to teach) and wonders (to overwhelm).
- **chen** (חֵן, v. 3): "favor, grace" — YHWH gives the Israelites favor in Egyptian eyes. This divine gift of chen transforms the social dynamic: the slaves become honored, the prophet becomes "very great" (gadol me'od). The despoiling of Egypt is not by force but by grace-enabled asking.
